cards.front: Name the 4 main wireless network types and their scope.
cards.back: WPAN (Personal, ~10m, e.g., Bluetooth). WLAN (Local, ~50m indoor, e.g., Wi-Fi 802.11). WMAN (Metropolitan, e.g., WiMAX). WWAN (Wide-area, e.g., 4G/5G cellular).

cards.front: What does CSMA/CA do, and why does Wi-Fi use it instead of CSMA/CD?
cards.back: Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision AVOIDANCE. Wi-Fi can't detect collisions while transmitting (radios are half-duplex), so it tries to AVOID them: listen, wait random backoff, optionally use RTS/CTS handshake before sending. Acks confirm receipt; missed acks trigger retransmission.

cards.front: List the main 802.11 standards and their key bands/speeds.
cards.back: 802.11b: 2.4 GHz, 11 Mbps. 802.11a: 5 GHz, 54 Mbps. 802.11g: 2.4 GHz, 54 Mbps. 802.11n (Wi-Fi 4): 2.4/5 GHz, ~600 Mbps, MIMO. 802.11ac (Wi-Fi 5): 5 GHz, multi-Gbps. 802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6): 2.4/5/6 GHz, OFDMA, 9.6 Gbps theoretical.

cards.front: How many non-overlapping channels does 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi have in the US, and which are they?
cards.back: 3 non-overlapping channels: 1, 6, 11 (each 22 MHz wide; spaced 25 MHz apart). The band has 11 channels total in the US (1-11), 13 in Europe — but only 3 don't overlap. Always design WLANs around 1/6/11 to avoid interference.

cards.front: What's the difference between Ad-hoc, Infrastructure, and Mesh wireless modes?
cards.back: Ad-hoc (IBSS): peer-to-peer, no AP. Infrastructure (BSS/ESS): clients connect via APs to wired LAN — standard model. Mesh: APs interconnect wirelessly to backhaul, extending coverage without dragging Ethernet to each AP.

cards.front: What are BSS, ESS, BSSID, and SSID?
cards.back: BSS: Basic Service Set — coverage of a single AP. BSSID: BSS Identifier — typically the AP's radio MAC address. ESS: Extended Service Set — multiple APs sharing the same SSID, allowing clients to roam. SSID: Service Set Identifier — the network's human-readable name.

cards.front: What does an AP do at L2 between wireless and wired sides?
cards.back: The AP bridges the wireless BSS to a wired VLAN. Frames going wireless→wired are translated from 802.11 → 802.3 Ethernet (and vice versa). The wired interface is typically a trunk carrying multiple VLANs, one per SSID.

cards.front: 5 RF propagation effects: Absorption, Reflection, Refraction, Diffraction, Scattering — what is each?
cards.back: Absorption: signal converted to heat, weakening it (walls, water, body). Reflection: signal bounces off (metal, mirrors). Refraction: signal bends when entering a different medium. Diffraction: signal bends around an obstacle's edge. Scattering: signal disperses in many directions (rough surfaces, dust). All affect Wi-Fi range and reliability.

cards.front: Properties of 2.4 GHz vs 5 GHz Wi-Fi bands?
cards.back: 2.4 GHz: longer reach, better penetration of walls/obstacles, BUT only 3 non-overlapping channels (1, 6, 11 in US) and crowded (microwave ovens, Bluetooth, baby monitors). 5 GHz: shorter reach, more attenuated by walls, BUT many more non-overlapping channels (~24 in US, depending on DFS) and less crowded. 6 GHz (added in Wi-Fi 6E / 802.11ax): even more spectrum, even shorter reach.

cards.front: Map 802.11 amendment letters to Wi-Fi marketing names: 802.11n, 802.11ac, 802.11ax.
cards.back: 802.11n = Wi-Fi 4 (2.4/5 GHz, MIMO). 802.11ac = Wi-Fi 5 (5 GHz only). 802.11ax = Wi-Fi 6 / Wi-Fi 6E (2.4/5/6 GHz, OFDMA). The Wi-Fi Alliance retroactively numbered older standards: 802.11g = 'Wi-Fi 3' is rarely used in marketing.

cards.front: AP modes: Repeater, Workgroup Bridge (WGB), Outdoor Bridge — what is each used for?
cards.back: Repeater: extends a BSS by re-transmitting another AP's signal (halves throughput). Workgroup Bridge (WGB): operates as a wireless CLIENT of another AP, then bridges WIRED devices on its Ethernet ports onto the wireless LAN. Outdoor Bridge: dedicated point-to-point or point-to-multipoint link between buildings/sites, often with directional antennas.

cards.front: Mesh wireless terms: MBSS, RAP, MAP, DS — what is each?
cards.back: MBSS (Mesh Basic Service Set): a mesh wireless network where APs interconnect over wireless backhaul. RAP (Root Access Point): the mesh AP that's connected to the wired network — gateway between mesh and the rest of the LAN. MAP (Mesh Access Point): non-root mesh AP, no wired uplink, communicates only wirelessly. DS (Distribution System): the upstream wired network all APs ultimately connect to.

cards.front: What does the SSID identify, and must it be unique?
cards.back: SSID (Service Set Identifier) is the human-readable name of a wireless network (e.g., 'CorpWiFi'). Up to 32 bytes. SSIDs do NOT have to be unique — two completely separate networks can share the name (clients distinguish by BSSID, the AP's radio MAC). One ESS = one SSID across multiple APs to allow seamless roaming.
